# Source Card: Growth Faltering and Failure to Thrive in Children

## Summary
This 2023 American Family Physician clinical review defines the anthropometric diagnostic criteria and monitoring approach for growth faltering (formerly "failure to thrive") in children. For the pediatric CDS module it establishes source-supported percentile/z-score triggers (< 5th percentile or a drop of two percentile lines; z-score decrease >= 1), the chart-selection rule (WHO < 2 years with gestational-age adjustment, CDC 2-20 years), a recovery target (z-score -2 to -1 without supplementation), and infant monitoring cadences. It is a secondary synthesis of CDC/WHO/ASPEN primary guidance.

## Key evidence (with exact-passage locators)
- (ev_001) Diagnosis when weight/weight-for-length/BMI is below the 5th percentile OR drops by two percentile lines — locator: Diagnosis — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:2771c2c9b138c900a6a54e175b80beefdbeff2ab9e8377e0667bcb9a264612fe]"
- (ev_002) z-score decrease of 1 or more predicts likely growth faltering (except compensatory in LGA) — locator: Diagnosis — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:589f6a80ad33dba47c19f7ef7e8a242b8bc20bb346b65ca6f13af04f5e4ccc23]"
- (ev_003) WHO charts (gestational-age adjusted) for < 2 years; CDC charts for 2-20 years — locator: Diagnosis — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:f4b43a1b5ed264c731403f7d3e91b98db0e6da7d7a8c10700afd5d9ff2b8a0a2]"
- (ev_004) Recovery target z-score -2 to -1 sustained without supplementation — locator: Further Evaluation and Treatment — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:c52bd2c30d03b037b42263a6fa61bbc7102347c28ade47f0909373b3412873d8]"
- (ev_005) Monitoring cadence: weekly (1-6 mo), every other week (6-12 mo) — locator: Further Evaluation and Treatment — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:bfa5c97ca42205fbc5cdd786129c774abc154d01d32bda3fdccdb57e2aaa626b]"
- (ev_006) Negative z-scores = lower percentiles; lower = more severe (directional convention) — locator: Diagnosis — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:b929d3ba6718f3d63ef602282f6ec666c39b032f9b80f32a0cfc4f2d2153f584]"

## Population / assay / lifecycle notes
- Age bands are explicit and chart-dependent: < 2 years uses WHO charts with gestational-age (corrected-age) adjustment; 2-20 years uses CDC charts. The choice of reference chart is a method dependency — the same measurement yields different percentiles/z-scores on WHO vs CDC references.
- Monitoring cadences are age-banded to infancy (1-6 mo weekly; 6-12 mo biweekly); the source does not state cadence for children > 12 months.
- Guideline version: Am Fam Physician 2023;107(6):597-603 (effective 2023-06). No retirement or supersession stated.

## Scope-exit / referral relevance (if any)
- The percentile/z-score triggers (ev_001, ev_002) and the failure-to-recover condition implicit in the recovery target (ev_004) function as case-identification / escalation signals for growth faltering. The source frames these as diagnostic thresholds, not as an executable referral rule; any CDS referral trigger must be authored downstream, not read directly from this card.

## Limitations & conflicts
- GAP (threshold not locatable): The malnutrition severity classification by z-score (reported in the run's key_points as mild = -1 to -2, moderate = -2 to -3, severe = -3 or below) is contained in Figure 1 of this source, which is NOT reproduced as text on the AAFP HTML page. No verbatim passage carrying those numeric ranges could be copied, so those cutoffs are NOT asserted here. To assert severity cutoffs, obtain the primary source (ASPEN/AAP consensus, referenced as Figure 1 refs 2 and 6) or the article PDF/figure.
- Secondary source: this is a clinical review synthesizing primary CDC/WHO/ASPEN guidance; primary sources should be preferred for authoritative cutoffs where a conflict arises.
- No conflicts with other sources recorded at ingestion time.
